design_philosophy.core_loop: Pilot a giant mech through tactical real-time combat, manage heat/damage systems, customize loadouts between missions, execute mission objectives across multiple planetary battlefields
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Thermal management during sustained combat vs. offensive firepower; managing mech physical simulation (independent leg/torso control) while tracking enemy positions and friendly forces
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a massive war machine in a sci-fi battlefield, customizing it to suit your playstyle, engaging in ideological conflict between rival factions (Crusaders vs. Wardens)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Commits fully to tactical simulation depth over accessibility—offers multiple control schemes from basic to advanced (independent leg/torso management), forces players to understand heat dissipation mechanics, weapon heat output, and fusion engine catastrophic failure risks. PC version emphasizes simulation realism; console ports deliberately simplify to arcade-style action, indicating intentional design philosophy split by platform

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Significant agency through mech customization between missions (weapon selection, armor, engine, heat sinks); player can choose control mode complexity (basic point-and-shoot to advanced independent limb control); player can override emergency thermal shutdown at risk of catastrophic engine detonation

progression.upgrade_system: Between-mission mech lab customization: weapon loadouts, armor values, engine selection, heat sink configuration. Implied progression through availability of different mech chassis and components across campaign missions
progression.unlock_mechanism: Mission-based progression; player joins either Clan Wolf or Clan Jade Falcon at start, determining campaign narrative branch. Later missions unlock squad command capabilities
replayability_hooks: Two distinct campaign paths (Clan Wolf vs. Clan Jade Falcon sides)
Mech customization variations creating different playstyle approaches
Multiple mission objective types (search-and-destroy, reconnaissance, base strike)
Expansion packs with additional content (Ghost Bear's Legacy, Mercenaries standalone)

world_building_techniques: Rendered cinematic introduction sequence (Digital Domain production)
Faction choice at campaign start establishes narrative stakes (Crusader vs. Warden ideology)
Mission briefings tied to ongoing Refusal War conflict and clan politics
BattleTech franchise lore integration (Star League Defense Force history, Kerensky legacy, clan isolation period)
Canonical fictional extrasolar planets as mission locations
Character-driven conflict (Ulric Kerensky treason accusation, Jade Falcon-Wolf rivalry)

faction_indicators: Clan Wolf: Warden ideology (protectionist, opposed to aggressive conquest)
Clan Jade Falcon: Crusader ideology (expansionist, seeks return to Inner Sphere dominance)
ComStar organization: neutral arbiter (opposes both clan factions)
danger_signals: Ulric Kerensky's treason accusation reveals internal clan conflict and political vulnerability
Crusader faction's willingness to challenge Truce of Tukayyid indicates destabilization
Refusal War as civil conflict within clan structure shows institutional breakdown
environmental_storytelling: Multiple fictional extrasolar planets serving as theaters of war
Mech customization reflecting clan aesthetic and technological choices
Mission objectives (search-and-destroy, reconnaissance, base strikes) reflect ongoing territorial conflict
